aliases: sweet potato yam coconut milk dessert
摩摩喳喳 (Mó Mo Zhā Zhā)

english: sweet potato yam coconut milk dessert
chinese: 摩摩喳喳 (Mó Mo Zhā Zhā)

food_subgroup: Coconut milk dessert soup with multiple root vegetables and sago pearls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Bubur Cha Cha or Peranakan coconut dessert soups. No defensible exact URI available; closest parent would be a generic coconut-milk-based dessert preparation, which is not formally coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sweet soups and porridges — closest FoodEx2 parent category for coconut milk dessert soups with root vegetables. Used as analogous parent; no exact Bubur Cha Cha entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bubur Cha Cha. A0CKP (sweet soups/porridges) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not encode specific Southeast Asian dessert preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bubur Cha Cha is approximately 250 g, providing around 255 kcal, 42.5 g carbohydrate, and 9.25 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Bubur Cha Cha — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo / IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite data for Bubur Cha Cha. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values represent a standard preparation with yam, orange sweet potato, purple sweet potato, sago pearls, coconut milk, and sugar. Minor micronutrient values (selenium, phosphorus, zinc, magnesium) are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for individual ingredients where direct composite data was unavailable.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Potato, Taro, and Sago — University of Sydney GI Database composite estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Bubur Cha Cha exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The composite GI of ~52 is estimated from the weighted average of the four primary starch sources: orange sweet potato (~44, University of Sydney GI Database), purple sweet potato (~50, peer-reviewed estimate), yam/taro (~54, University of Sydney GI Database), and sago pearls (~70, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighting reflects approximate ingredient proportions in a standard preparation. GL of 22.1 is calculated per 250 g serving (42.5 g available carbohydrate × GI 52 / 100). GI category is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). Note: the GL of 22.1 per bowl is in the high GL range (GL > 20), which is clinically relevant for T2DM management despite the low GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (35 mg/100 g; 88 mg per 250 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Bubur Cha Cha is low in sodium at 35 mg/100 g (88 mg per standard bowl), well within the recommended limit for hypertensive individuals, and the potassium content (120 mg/100 g; 300 mg per bowl) provides a modest vasodilatory benefit; it is generally suitable for hypertensive patients in standard portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate composite GI (~52) but high glycemic load per bowl (GL ~22.1)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although Bubur Cha Cha has a low composite GI of approximately 52 due to the mixed root vegetable starch structures, the glycemic load per standard 250 g bowl is approximately 22.1 (high GL threshold > 20), driven by 42.5 g of available carbohydrate from sweet potato, yam, sago pearls, and added sugar; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to half a bowl (~125 g) to keep GL below 12, and should avoid adding extra sugar or condensed milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (3.2 g/100 g; 8 g per bowl) — predominantly medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat in Bubur Cha Cha (3.2 g/100 g; 8 g per 250 g bowl) is derived entirely from coconut milk and consists predominantly of medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs, mainly lauric acid), which have a different metabolic profile from long-chain saturated fats; however, individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should still moderate intake to one bowl per occasion, as total saturated fat contribution remains clinically relevant at higher consumption frequencies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytonutrients: beta-carotene (orange sweet potato) and anthocyanins (purple sweet potato)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bubur Cha Cha provides meaningful anti-inflammatory phytonutrients — beta-carotene from orange sweet potato (85 mcg RAE/100 g) and anthocyanins from purple sweet potato — alongside magnesium (18 mg/100 g; 45 mg per bowl) which supports inflammatory pathway regulation; it is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and is entirely plant-based with zero cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubur Cha Cha is a beloved Peranakan (Baba-Nyonya) coconut milk dessert soup originating from Penang and Singapore, widely enjoyed across Malaysia, Singapore, and the broader Southeast Asian Straits Chinese community. The dish consists of soft cubes of yam (taro), orange sweet potato, and purple sweet potato simmered in a fragrant, lightly sweetened coconut milk broth, with translucent sago pearls and sometimes grass jelly or sweet potato vermicelli added for textural variety. The name 'cha cha' is onomatopoeic, evoking the soft, wobbly, bouncy texture of the ingredients. The visual presentation is striking — orange, purple, and white pieces floating in a creamy white broth — making it one of the most photogenic Southeast Asian desserts. Nutritionally, Bubur Cha Cha provides 102 kcal per 100 g (255 kcal per standard 250 g bowl), with 17 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g fat (predominantly coconut milk MCT saturated fat at 3.2 g), and 0.9 g protein per 100 g. The phytonutrient profile is notable: orange sweet potato contributes beta-carotene (85 mcg RAE/100 g), purple sweet potato contributes anthocyanins with anti-inflammatory properties, and yam contributes vitamin B6 and potassium. The composite glycemic index is approximately 52 (low GI), significantly better than glutinous-rice-based kuih such as kuih seri muka (GI ~75+), but the glycemic load per bowl is approximately 22.1 (high GL), driven by the 42.5 g total carbohydrate load. Clinically, Bubur Cha Cha is an acceptable occasional dessert for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes if portion is limited to half a bowl (~125 g, GL ~11). It is naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is served warm or cold and is commonly found at Peranakan restaurants, Malay kuih stalls, and during Hari Raya celebrations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD, both approved primary regional sources for Singaporean and Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2. The GI estimate is derived from University of Sydney GI Database values for the individual component starches (sweet potato, taro, sago), which is the standard analogous estimation methodology for composite dishes without a direct GI measurement. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Bubur Cha Cha is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central does not have a direct composite entry for this dish. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite coconut milk dessert soup is not reported in the available approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): coconut milk and plant-based root vegetables contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Bubur Cha Cha is entirely plant-based; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore data. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present in this preparation. 7. GI of 52 is a composite analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values; no direct GI measurement for Bubur Cha Cha exists in the published literature as of 2026. 8. Two portion sizes appear in the source data (250 g and 300 g); the 250 g serving is used as the default for GL calculation, consistent with the food name description. The 300 g serving would yield GL ~26.5. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible parent (sweet soups and porridges); no exact FoodEx2 code for Bubur Cha Cha exists.
